stories.title: $563M Palmetto Hospital Reaches Construction Milestone
stories.excerpt: BayCare Health System has topped out its $563 million hospital in Palmetto. The 463,000-square-foot facility is slated to open near the end of 2028. The Tampa Bay Business Journal reports the hospital will include 154 private patient rooms and can expand to over 200 beds as demand accelerates. Emergency care, general and specialty surgery, cardiology, gastroenterology, orthopedics and diagnostic and interventional imaging will all be available as will a neonatal intensive care unit. Last April, the system broke ground on the project on Moccasin Wallow Road. Robins & Morton is the general contractor. A 46,000-square-foot BayCare HealthHub is also set to open on the campus in late 2026. HealthHubs are specialized, one-stop centers spread across the region offering primary care, specialty services, physical therapy, imaging and digital health tools in one location. The post $563M Palmetto Hospital Reaches Construction Milestone appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: BayCare Health System topped out $563M, 463K-SF hospital in Palmetto, FL opening late 2028.
154-bed facility with expansion potential to 200+ beds; 46K-SF HealthHub opening late 2026.
Major healthcare anchor near Tampa Bay signals sustained population and employment growth trajectory.
stories.summary: BayCare Health System has reached topping-out on a $563 million, 463,000-square-foot hospital in Palmetto, Florida, scheduled to open in late 2028 with 154 private beds expandable to 200+. The project includes emergency care, cardiology, orthopedics, NICU, and a separate 46,000-square-foot HealthHub opening in late 2026. Robins & Morton is the general contractor.
stories.impact: Large-scale healthcare infrastructure investment in Palmetto (south Tampa Bay exurb) signals sustained confidence in population and employment growth. This anchor will attract service workers, support staff, and professionals over the next 3–5 years, strengthening rental demand in nearby submarkets before and immediately after opening; developers should prioritize workforce housing (100–150 units, mixed-income) within 2–3 miles of the hospital site to capture demand from nurses, technicians, and administrative staff.

stories.title: Opryland Resort Continues to Grow
stories.excerpt: The massive Nashville hotel-and-entertainment Mecca is getting even more massive. The Gaylord Opryland Resort & Convention Center features 2,888 guest rooms, 20 restaurants, nine acres of indoor atriums featuring lush plants, winding pathways, and waterfalls with flatboat rides inside the hotel. Amenities include indoor and outdoor swimming pools, and a full-service spa. Recently, Ryman Hospitality reached a milestone in topping off its $131 million expansion of the popular attraction. The expansion will add 108,000 square feet of meeting space to the property. It includes a 31,000-square-foot ballroom, 38,000 square feet of breakout space and 39,000 square feet of pre-function space. The project is set to be complete in the second quarter of 2027 which will be Opryland’s 50th birthday. The expansion is part of a larger $224 million project at Opryland, that also included a $53 million renovation of existing meeting space and the $40 million transformation of the Magnolia Pool Courtyard, which includes a 550-seat sports bar, Foundry Fieldhouse Sports Bar & Taproom. The post Opryland Resort Continues to Grow appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ryman Hospitality topped out $131M expansion at Gaylord Opryland Resort, Nashville; completing Q2 2027.
108K-SF meeting space addition (31K ballroom, 38K breakout, 39K pre-function) for convention market.
Part of larger $224M project including $53M meeting renovation and $40M Magnolia Pool Courtyard overhaul.
stories.summary: Ryman Hospitality reached topping-out on a $131 million, 108,000-square-foot expansion of the Gaylord Opryland Resort & Convention Center in Nashville, with completion expected in Q2 2027. The expansion adds a 31,000-square-foot ballroom, 38,000 square feet of breakout space, and 39,000 square feet of pre-function space. This is part of a broader $224 million capital program that also includes a $53 million meeting-space renovation and a $40 million Magnolia Pool Courtyard transformation.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. Opryland expansion is hotel/convention-focused and does not directly drive residential demand. However, Nashville's continued status as a major convention destination may attract some hospitality-adjacent service workers and temporary residents; the real demand signal is in ancillary office, retail, and food-service employment, not a primary multifamily driver.

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Opinion essay on Manhattan developer psychology and risk tolerance; no quantitative market data provided.
Anecdotal reference to MetroLoft's Nathan Berman Pfizer HQ office-to-residential conversion with partial collapse.
Feature lacks specifics on market conditions, financing, supply, or demand implications.
stories.summary: An opinion essay reflecting on developer mentality in Manhattan, using Harry Macklowe as a legend and Nathan Berman's MetroLoft (converting former Pfizer HQ to rentals) as a counterpoint. The article notes Berman's conversion experienced a partial structural collapse and includes a quote from real estate appraiser Jonathan Miller on developer personality types. No hard data, numbers, or market implications provided.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. The piece is editorial commentary on developer risk appetite and psychology, not a market signal. The Pfizer HQ conversion collapse is a construction/structural issue specific to one adaptive-reuse project, not a signal of broader office-to-residential viability in Manhattan or other markets.

stories.title: Amazon Rents 1.2M-SF Atlanta-Area Warehouse
stories.excerpt: Amazon leased 1,219,826 square feet at 1305 Highway 42 South, 20 miles southeast of Atlanta. PNK Group USA developed the site that was delivered in March. The Atlanta Business Chronicle reports the lease is likely the town’s largest so far this year. In addition to Amazon, PNK Group has attracted other tenants to its Henry County complex. Those include mattress maker Purple Innovation and water treatment and sanitation company Ecolab. JLL represents PNK Group in leasing. Atlanta’s industrial market shows net absorption improving year over year to 2.6 million square feet in the black, per CBRE‘s second-quarter report. Leasing activity totaled 15.1 million square feet in Q2. On its website, PNK Group says it is an investor and vertically integrated developer of multifunctional industrial buildings. As a full-cycle engineering, manufacturing, and development company, PNK Group develops its own technological innovations. The post Amazon Rents 1.2M-SF Atlanta-Area Warehouse appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Amazon leased 1.22M SF at Highway 42 South, 20 miles SE of Atlanta; delivered March 2024.
PNK Group developed site; tenants include Purple Innovation and Ecolab; JLL represents leasing.
Atlanta industrial market absorbed 2.6M SF net positive in H1 2024; leasing totaled 15.1M SF in Q2.
stories.summary: Amazon leased 1,219,826 square feet at a PNK Group-developed warehouse in Henry County, Georgia (20 miles southeast of Atlanta), delivered in March 2024. The site also attracted Purple Innovation and Ecolab as tenants. Atlanta's industrial market showed strong H1 2024 performance with 2.6 million square feet of net positive absorption and 15.1 million square feet of leasing activity in Q2.
stories.impact: Amazon's large logistics footprint in Henry County signals sustained ecommerce/3PL demand and supply-chain investment in the Atlanta exurbs. This drives intra-metro migration of warehouse and logistics workers toward Henry County and surrounding areas, creating rental demand for workforce housing (workforce/affordable segments) in closer-in submarkets and near transit corridors to Henry County. Developers should monitor workforce demographics and wage bands for logistics employment in south and southeast Atlanta suburbs.

stories.title: Grover Corlew Lands Refi on 223K-SF Deerfield Beach Office Park
stories.excerpt: Grover Corlew has refinanced Hillsboro Center, a 223,716-square-foot office park in Deerfield Beach, Fla. A two-year, $30 million senior loan was originated by BankUnited and arranged by JLL Capital Markets. Commercial Search reports Grover Corlew acquired the two-building office park in 2019 for $32.5 million from TriGate Capital. The office park was completed in the 1980s. Grover Corlew has invested $10 million over the past three years to renovate and reposition the asset. The 97% leased office park has one six-story building at 600 W. Hillsboro Blvd., totaling roughly 116,250 square feet, and one two-story building totaling about 99,850 square feet. Grover Corlew plans to redevelop a portion of the site with a 360-unit multifamily community known as Maya Hillsboro. The JLL Capital Markets Debt Advisory team that arranged the financing was led by Paul Stasaitis and Paul Adams. The post Grover Corlew Lands Refi on 223K-SF Deerfield Beach Office Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Grover Corlew refinanced 223.7K-SF Deerfield Beach office park; $30M two-year loan from BankUnited.
97% leased; acquired 2019 for $32.5M, invested $10M renovations; plans 360-unit multifamily (Maya Hillsboro).
Office-to-residential conversion project; refinance signals confidence in redevelopment feasibility and multifamily demand.
stories.summary: Grover Corlew refinanced Hillsboro Center, a 223,716-square-foot office park in Deerfield Beach, Florida, with a $30 million two-year senior loan from BankUnited arranged by JLL Capital Markets. The asset is 97% leased and was acquired in 2019 for $32.5 million; Corlew has invested $10 million in renovations. The developer plans to redevelop a portion of the site into Maya Hillsboro, a 360-unit multifamily community.
stories.impact: Office-to-residential conversion is being financed in South Florida (Deerfield Beach, Broward County), signaling lender comfort with adaptive reuse and multifamily development fundamentals despite lingering office headwinds. The refinance de-risks the conversion and validates strong rental demand in the Deerfield Beach submarket. Watch for similar office conversions and mixed-use strategies in Broward and Palm Beach as offices struggle with legacy leases and vacancy; this is a proof-of-concept for capital recycling in a mature South Florida market.

stories.title: New York Enacting Nation’s First Statewide Data Center Moratorium
stories.excerpt: Commercial real estate’s booming data center industry is suddenly not in an Empire State of mind. New York Gov. Kathy Hochul is slated to sign an executive order Tuesday morning enacting the nation’s first statewide moratorium on construction of the large-scale data center facilities that have been powering the country’s increased usage of AI. The move, which includes a one-year ban on new data centers with at least 50 megawatts, also directs state regulators to create new standards for addressing environmental, energy and water-use impacts. “As data center development threatens to hike up utility bills, deplete our natural resources, and create uncertainty for New Yorkers, it’s my responsibility to take action and lead,” Hochul said in a statement. Hochul’s order comes five weeks after the New York State Legislature passed a bill that would also impose a one-year data center moratorium in an attempt to create more guardrails. Maine lawmakers attempted a similar data center moratorium this past spring before the legislation was vetoed by Gov. Janet Mills. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: NY Governor Hochul signing executive order Tuesday enacting nation's first statewide data center moratorium.
One-year ban on data centers ≥50 megawatts; directs regulators to create standards for energy/water/environmental impact.
Legislature passed companion bill; Maine lawmakers also pursuing moratorium; impacts AI infrastructure investment in NY.
stories.summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul is enacting the nation's first statewide data center moratorium via executive order, imposing a one-year ban on construction of facilities with 50+ megawatts of capacity. The move directs state regulators to establish new standards for environmental, energy, and water-use impacts. It follows passage of a companion legislative bill and reflects growing regulatory pushback on AI-driven data center expansion.
stories.impact: Data center moratorium in New York reduces immediate industrial/special-purpose real estate demand in the state and shifts AI infrastructure investment to other markets (Texas, Virginia, Ohio, others). For NY-based multifamily developers, this removes a significant source of near-term job creation and population in-migration from data center construction and operations. However, it does not directly affect residential demand unless it cascades into broader tech industry pullback or slowdown; monitor whether this prompts major cloud/AI employers to reduce NY footprints or pause hiring.

stories.title: June CPI Decelerates from May, Driven by Drop in Energy Index
stories.excerpt: U.S. inflation decelerated in June, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) declining 0.4% after increasing 0.5% in the previous month. A 5.7% drop in the energy index drove the monthly all-items decrease, more than offsetting increases in indexes for shelter and food, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said Tuesday. The energy index was up 15.7% on an annual basis. The monthly decline followed increases of 3.9% in May, 3.8% in April and 10.9% in March. This decline in the all-items index was the largest monthly decrease since April 2020, when it fell 0.8%. On a year-over-year basis, the CPI climbed 3.5%, below the consensus estimate of 3.8% as well as the May annual increase of 4.2%. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, was unchanged from May, while the Y-O-Y increase of 2.6% came in below a consensus estimate of 2.9%. Indexes that decreased over the month include motor vehicle insurance, communication, apparel, medical care, and used cars and trucks. Conversely, the indexes for recreation, household furnishings and operations, and personal care increased in June. The food index maintained the May monthly increase of 0.2%. The shelter index rose 0.1% in June, below the May level of 0.3%. Both the CPI release and the Producer Price Index announcement on Wednesday occur ahead of Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh’s appearances before the House and Senate. Warsh is scheduled to appear before the House Financial Services Committee at 10 a.m. EDT on Tuesday, and before the Senate Banking Committee at the same time on Wednesday. The post June CPI Decelerates from May, Driven by Drop in Energy Index appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: June CPI fell 0.4% month-over-month; Y-O-Y increase of 3.5%, below May's 4.2%.
Core inflation flat month-over-month; Y-O-Y core of 2.6% vs. consensus 2.9%.
Energy index down 5.7% monthly; shelter index rose only 0.1% month-over-month, below May's 0.3%.
stories.summary: U.S. Consumer Price Index declined 0.4% month-over-month in June, the largest monthly decrease since April 2020, driven by a 5.7% drop in the energy index. Year-over-year CPI slowed to 3.5% from May's 4.2%, below consensus estimates. Core inflation (ex-food/energy) remained unchanged month-over-month, with year-over-year core inflation of 2.6%, also below consensus.
stories.impact: Moderating inflation, particularly in shelter (rent) components, suggests Fed may pursue rate cuts in coming months, improving financing conditions and cap rates for multifamily acquisitions and development. Lower energy and shelter inflation reduces construction cost pressures and supports tenant affordability/demand, especially in rent-sensitive segments. Watch for Fed Chair's testimony this week (post-article publication) as a catalyst for capital markets re-pricing; developers should prepare refinancing and takeout strategies in anticipation of lower SOFR and reduced debt service costs.

stories.title: RealPage Acquires Cherre in Major Proptech Deal
stories.excerpt: Multifamily software and data analytics giant RealPage announced Tuesday that it has acquired institutional portfolio intelligence firm Cherre. Richardson, Texas-based RealPage acquired Cherre in a cash and money deal, said Dirk Wakeham, president and CEO of RealPage. Wakeham declined to provide specific figures on the acquisition. Manhattan-based Cherre will retain its brand as part of RealPage, said L.D. Salmanson, co-founder and now the former CEO of Cherre. Salmanson will remain with Cherre as the company’s senior vice president, he said. “We are not going away — in fact, the very opposite,” Salmanson said of Cherre, a data platform that connects decision-makers to property investment, management and market information. “We will continue to grow the RealPage ecosystem and continue to grow with our clients. I’m very excited about that. My role essentially is the same, which is to help insurance companies in the world have access to governed trusted data to be able to power their agentic future. And now we have an extra continued mission together, which is to bring that across the entire capital stack, the entire ecosystem, and leverage the existing RealPage product line, which is very broad.” Founded in 2016, Cherre’s platform involves unifying and analyzing more than 4 billion entities and $4 trillion in real assets globally to create a vetted and compliant environment for institutional owners and investors. In acquiring Cherre, RealPage customers will gain access to a layer of intelligence built specifically to bridge property-level data with portfolio- and fund-level context, according to the announcement. “This was more about the value that this brings to our data,” said Wakeham. “We’ve got 25 petabytes [a million gigabytes] of data, and the opportunity for the 1 plus 1 equals 3 here is pointing the Cherre technology, their unified data models and data, against our 25 petabytes of data that’s largely unorganized and somewhat unusable.” In be
bullets: RealPage acquired Manhattan-based Cherre (portfolio intelligence platform); cash and equity deal, no price disclosed.
Cherre retains brand under RealPage; co-founder L.D. Salmanson stays as SVP; data analytics consolidation.
Multifamily software/data market consolidation; RealPage strengthens competitive moat in property analytics and AI.
stories.summary: RealPage announced the acquisition of Cherre, a Manhattan-based institutional portfolio intelligence firm specializing in property investment, management, and market data. The deal was structured as cash and equity; Cherre will retain its brand and Salmanson will remain as senior vice president. No financial terms were disclosed.
stories.impact: Proptech consolidation around RealPage reinforces vertical integration and data dominance in multifamily analytics, underwriting, and operational intelligence. Developers benefit from unified data ecosystems (Cherre + RealPage platforms) for market analysis, competitive benchmarking, and asset management, reducing fragmentation and improving decision speed. This is not a direct supply/demand/capital signal but signals ongoing capital attraction to multifamily software and data assets, validating the sector's importance to portfolio management and capital efficiency.

stories.title: Noda Launches Agentic AI for Commercial Building Operations
stories.excerpt: Noda, a leading artificial intelligence startup for commercial building operations, has launched what it calls the first agentic AI providing 24/7 automation to help ameliorate the industry-wide shortage of facility operators. The Washington, D.C.-based company rebranded to its present form in 2023, with the goal of optimizing building operations by mapping data points, automating tasks and driving actionable insights, said Kate Henningsen, CEO of Noda. “This technology was only about 32 percent accurate a year ago,” said Henningsen. “Although people are doing AI in buildings, we have not run across anybody who’s tackling the workflows of building engineers on site. I think it was probably impossible to do this over a year or 18 months ago, and we haven’t seen anybody who was aiding the actions of building operators and engineers in this way.” That created a business opportunity. “Every building in a portfolio is leaving net operating income (NOI) on the table right now, because no team has the capacity to find and fix operational waste at the speed and scale the problem demands,” said Henningsen. “Noda changes that equation. Our AI runs alongside the team you already have, doing the work of a full engineering organization across every asset, every hour of every day — and the impact shows up directly on the profit and loss statement.” Noda’s agentic AI platform creates a fundamental shift in how owners and operators run their buildings and portfolios, the company claims. Unlike analytics tools that surface problems for staff to investigate, Noda’s platform finds, fixes and verifies the problem and its financial impact across every asset, without requiring human intervention. The company has a portfolio of 1,200 buildings, totaling 350 million square feet, based on Salesforce data, said Henningsen. “We’re doing a lot of the building engineering and facilities management operations with new tools,” she said. “There’s great agentic ways to
bullets: Noda.ai (D.C.-based) launched agentic AI for 24/7 building operations automation; addresses operator shortage.
CEO Kate Henningsen: accuracy improved from ~32% one year ago; targets operational waste reduction in NOI.
Targets facility engineer/operator workflows; aims to unlock NOI currently left on table due to staffing constraints.
stories.summary: Noda, a Washington, D.C.-based AI startup for commercial building operations, launched its first agentic AI platform providing 24/7 automation to address an industry-wide shortage of facility operators. CEO Kate Henningsen noted the technology's accuracy improved from ~32% one year ago and targets operational workflows of building engineers. The platform aims to identify and fix operational waste and unlock NOI across property portfolios.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact on multifamily development supply/demand/capital. Building automation and NOI optimization tools primarily serve owners and operators post-delivery. However, if Noda's technology proves effective, it may reduce operational capex and improve NOI multiples for existing assets, potentially raising property valuations and improving exit returns for developers. Longer-term, labor-cost savings in building operations may compress NOI multiple spreads and cap rates as automation becomes standard, affecting underwriting assumptions for new multifamily projects.

stories.title: RXR, Waterfall, NYLife Finance $103M Recap of Englewood Cliffs Medical Campus
stories.excerpt: Scott Rechler’s RXR continues to make moves. RXR, Waterfall Asset Management and New York Life Investment Management will finance a $103 million recapitalization of Sylvan Corporate Center, a four-building, 276,000-square-foot medical office campus owned by the Andalex Group in Englewood Cliffs, N.J., Commercial Observer can first report. The new capital will refinance an existing mortgage and support a major expansion of the center’s existing footprint for anchor tenant Englewood Health, which has signed a 20-year lease extension and plans to double its footprint to 202,000 square feet, according to a release. The asset, which opened in 1998, is currently 100 percent leased. “This recapitalization, together with Englewood Health’s long-term expansion, validates the strength of the asset, the quality of the location and the long-term vision we have had for the property,” said Andrew Silverman, principal of Andalex, whose firm acquired Sylvan Corporate Center in 2004 and oversaw the construction of two additional buildings on-site by 2008. Englewood Health currently operates several buildings at Sylvan Corporate Center: an orthopedics and primary care offices, an Asian health and wellness center, and an ambulatory surgery center. The firm is expected to develop a 50,000-square-foot outpatient imaging and therapeutics center and a 50,000-square-foot physician office space as part of the new expansion. “Sylvan Corporate Center represents the type of high-quality, relationship-driven credit investment that RXR is well positioned to execute,” said Russ Young, head of investments at RXR. Englewood Health’s credit profile is also of note, as the provider is scheduled to merge with RWJ Barnabas Health, in a deal that would create the largest combined health care system in the state of New Jersey. Alexander Silverman, another principal at Andalex, called Englewood Health “an exceptional institution and an ideal long-term partner,” in a statement. “Their commitment to more th
bullets: RXR, Waterfall Asset Management, NYLife Investment Management financed $103M recapitalization of Sylvan Corporate Center.
Englewood Cliffs, NJ medical office campus (276K SF, four buildings); Englewood Health signed 20-year lease, doubling footprint to 202K SF.
Asset 100% leased; Andalex owner since 2004; refinance validates medical office strength and anchor tenant stability.
stories.summary: RXR, Waterfall Asset Management, and New York Life Investment Management structured a $103 million recapitalization of Sylvan Corporate Center, a four-building, 276,000-square-foot medical office campus in Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey. Anchor tenant Englewood Health signed a 20-year lease extension and plans to double its footprint to 202,000 square feet. The asset is 100% leased.
stories.impact: Medical office anchor expansion (Englewood Health doubling footprint) in Northern New Jersey signals sustained healthcare demand and employment growth in the region. This attracts clinical, administrative, and support staff, driving residential demand in nearby commuter corridors (Englewood Cliffs, Fort Lee, Hudson County). Developers should target workforce housing (200–500 units, mixed-income, transit-adjacent) in proximate Hudson/Bergen County submarkets to serve expanded medical employment base.

stories.title: Gaia CEO Danny Fishman Grew the Development Firm by Looking Way Ahead
stories.excerpt: Danny Fishman swims against the current. Unlike most companies during the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) — which from 2007 to 2009 triggered a worldwide economic downturn — Fishman, co-founder and CEO of GAIA Real Estate, took the opportunity to be extremely active in deal-making. Not only did he launch Gaia during the period, but he also hit his professional stride by acquiring distressed properties — properties no other company would dare buy during a financial crisis — for steep discounts, flipping them into desirable assets, and selling them for more than triple what they’re worth. “Danny’s been very successful in not falling where a lot of other people fell,” said Doron Broman, founder and CEO of Moderno Development Group and a frequent business partner of Gaia. “His strategy is very smart, and he’s very disciplined.” Fishman’s tactic of acquiring mispriced assets has turned Gaia, a private equity and property development firm focusing on acquiring, renovating and managing residential properties across the U.S., into a prominent institutional investor. All told, the firm — along with its privately held real estate investment trust Gaia REIT — has a portfolio encompassing more than 80 properties, 20,000 residential units and $4 billion in assets under management. But all of that success came after Fishman grew the firm’s humble roots. Fishman was born and raised in Jerusalem, launching an early career preparing large Israeli government-owned real estate companies for privatization. He then moved on to found Tamir Fishman, an Israeli investment bank and asset management firm that’s still growing today. Fishman eventually met his business partner and Gaia co-founder, Ken Woolley, in Israel around 2008. From there, Gaia was born, taking its name from Greek mythology. (Gaia is the Earth.) The co-founders hit it off and both saw what they called a “rare opportunity” at the time, which was to invest in apartments during what was going to be an epic downturn during the
bullets: Feature profile on GAIA Real Estate CEO Danny Fishman; company founded during 2007–2009 GFC.
Fishman's strategy: acquire distressed properties at steep discounts, renovate, flip for 3x+ returns.
GAIA is private equity/property development firm; focus on acquiring, renovating, managing residential across U.S.
stories.summary: A profile of Danny Fishman, co-founder and CEO of GAIA Real Estate, highlighting his contrarian strategy of aggressively acquiring distressed properties during the 2007–2009 Global Financial Crisis while competitors retreated. Fishman built GAIA into a prominent institutional investor through disciplined acquisition of underpriced assets, renovation, and value-add repositioning. GAIA specializes in acquiring, renovating, and managing residential properties across the U.S.
stories.impact: Limited immediate market signal. Feature is a retrospective on Fishman's success during a past crisis cycle. However, it underscores the value of disciplined capital deployment and contrarian positioning during market downturns. For current developers, this is a reminder that if capital markets tighten or recession looms, distressed-asset acquisition and value-add strategies may outperform greenfield development; monitor GAIA's current activity and positioning for signals of market stress or opportunity.

stories.title: Buyer scores $2M judgment against Mandarin Oriental Boca developer
stories.excerpt: The legal troubles at Penn-Florida’s Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton continue to mount after the developer lost a $2 million judgment.  A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge sided with Douglas and Debra Jacobs after they filed a lawsuit against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III, which alleged the developer failed to complete their condo in time, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The developer announced plans for the 248-unit hotel-condo Mandarin Oriental in 2015, with an initial opening target of 2017, the outlet reported. The complaint alleged that the Jacobses signed a contract to buy a condo in the development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge sided with Douglas and Debra Jacobs against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III; $2M judgment.
Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton (248-unit hotel-condo); condo not completed by Dec. 31, 2025 contract deadline.
Thirteen buyers have sued Via Mizner; only one judgment so far; another settlement for $2.1M; project severely delayed.
stories.summary: A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge awarded a $2 million judgment against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III in favor of condo buyers Douglas and Debra Jacobs. The developer failed to complete their unit by the December 31, 2025 deadline specified in their purchase contract, signed in 2022. Thirteen buyers have filed lawsuits against Via Mizner; one judgment and one $2.1 million settlement have been reached so far.
stories.impact: Penn-Florida's project delivery failures and litigation exposure in South Florida signal broader condo-market headwinds: buyer cancellations, delayed closings, and legal disputes reduce sales certainty and increase carry costs for sponsors. This weakens Penn-Florida's balance sheet and may force capital reallocation away from new starts. For competing developers in South Florida, this is a warning sign: rising construction costs, supply-chain delays, and labor shortages are making delivery timelines harder to hit. Developers should stress-test delivery schedules, embed contingency reserves, and consider longer presale windows and buyer-friendly closeout terms to avoid litigation and reputation damage in Miami/Boca Raton.

stories.title: Chicago proposes $287M TIF raid for Related Midwest’s megaproject The 78
stories.excerpt: Chicago officials are set to throw $287 million of property tax revenue into the megaproject that includes Major League Soccer team the Chicago Fire’s new stadium in the South Loop.  The proposal for the project known as the 78 is being considered by the Chicago City Council Finance Committee. The shift in funding is expected to partially reorganize a tax increment financing subsidy for the project’s developer, Related Midwest, according to Crain’s.  The revised agreement would add $287 million from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the TIF district that the 78 sits in, meaning the project’s developers would not have […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago proposes $287M TIF redirect to 'The 78' megaproject (Related Midwest); MLS soccer stadium, mixed-use.
Plan shifts Canal/Congress TIF funds to Roosevelt/Clark TIF zone; original plan was $85M over 3 years.
Alderman Bill Conway opposes raid; move could absorb all Canal/Congress TIF revenue (~$65M/year) for 9 years.
stories.summary: Chicago officials are proposing a $287 million redirection of property tax revenue from the Canal/Congress Tax Increment Financing district to the Roosevelt/Clark TIF district to fund 'The 78,' a Related Midwest megaproject in the South Loop that includes a new Chicago Fire MLS stadium and mixed-use development. The original TIF plan allocated $85 million over three years; the revised proposal could redirect nearly all of Canal/Congress's ~$65 million annual TIF revenue for the next nine years.
stories.impact: TIF capture and reallocation in Chicago favors Related Midwest's megaproject but starves competing developments in the Canal/Congress zone (including Union Station maintenance). Developers competing for TIF resources in Chicago face reduced incentive availability; those already committed to South Loop projects may benefit, while those targeting West Loop or other neighborhoods face tighter financing. The $287 million subsidy validates The 78's necessity for TIF support, suggesting market-rate returns are insufficient without public capture. Developers bidding on Chicago projects should monitor TIF allocation trends and incorporate longer closeout timelines for subsidy realization.

stories.title: Shvo gets fraud claims tossed in Mandarin Oriental suit
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Shvo is almost off the hook at the Mandarin Oriental.  A judge in New York Supreme Court last week dismissed claims brought against Shvo and his companies by two condo owners at his Mandarin Oriental Residences, who had alleged fraud and shoddy construction.  The judge’s dismissal likely takes some pressure off Shvo, who has been dealing with numerous lawsuits and disputes in the past year.  “We are pleased that the Court dismissed the overwhelming majority of plaintiffs’ claims, including all claims against Michael Shvo personally,” Shvo attorney Morris Missry said in a statement. “The decision confirms our long-held […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge dismissed fraud claims against developer Michael Shvo and his companies at Mandarin Oriental Residences, NYC.
Condos at 685 Fifth Avenue (former Gucci Building); condo owners alleged fraud, shoddy construction, undisclosed disputes.
Shvo faced dispute with Turkish investor Serdar Bilgili (settled 2020); prior litigation history but fraud claims dismissed.
stories.summary: A New York State Supreme Court judge dismissed the majority of claims brought by condo owners John and Diane Goodman against Michael Shvo and his companies at Mandarin Oriental Residences (685 Fifth Avenue, formerly the Gucci Building). The Goodmans alleged fraud, inadequate arbitration disclosures, and construction defects on their $6.1 million one-bedroom unit. Shvo had previously settled with Turkish investor Serdar Bilgili in 2020 over fund-misuse allegations.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact on multifamily development. Shvo's litigation wins and dismissals improve his litigation risk profile but do not signal broader market conditions affecting apartment supply or demand. However, the case illustrates residential condo market volatility and buyer litigation risk in luxury NYC condos; multifamily developers should note that condo conversion or condo-rental hybrid strategies carry execution and reputational risk, particularly in New York where buyer activism is high.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas, but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge tossed lawsuit by Soho Development (Roger Stein) against Two Roads Development over Four Seasons Bahamas.
Soho alleges exclusion from $300M Four Seasons luxury condo project (67 units); claims breach of NDA/non-circumvention.
Two Roads and Access Industries (Len Blavatnik) developing Ocean Club, Four Seasons Residences; presales underway; completion 2028.
stories.summary: A judge dismissed a lawsuit filed by Soho Development, led by Delray Beach attorney Roger Stein, against Two Roads Development alleging exclusion from a $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas. However, Soho claims the judge addressed the wrong complaint and says it will pursue further action. Two Roads and Access Real Estate (part of billionaire Len Blavatnik's Access Industries) are developing the 67-unit Ocean Club, Four Seasons Residences on Paradise Island, with presales underway and completion expected in 2028.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to U.S. multifamily development. The dispute is a partnership/NDA breach case in the Bahamas luxury condo market and does not directly affect domestic rental demand or capital. However, it signals high-stakes competition and partner disputes in ultra-luxury development, particularly in offshore/destination markets. U.S. developers should note that international partnership agreements and non-compete clauses require careful negotiation and legal vetting to avoid disputes that tie up capital and delay projects.

stories.title: Blake Poston jumps from the gridiron to the high-rise with plans for a $100M Uptown Dallas apartment project
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Poston (former SMU football player, Cover2 Capital founder) developing $100M, 20-story apartment tower in Dallas.
The Savannah: 100 luxury units (mix of 1–3 BR), three penthouse apartments; Uptown Dallas location (5220 McKinney).
Cover2 also developing Wheelhouse (297 units, North Richland Hills) and Greerhouse (garden-style, Anna); construction target late 2025.
stories.summary: Blake Poston, an SMU football alum and founder of Cover2 Capital, is developing The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury apartment tower with 100 units (mix of one- to three-bedroom plus three penthouses) in Uptown Dallas. The project is targeting construction start in late 2025 and is currently in design and city review. Cover2 is also behind Wheelhouse (297 units in North Richland Hills) and Greerhouse (garden-style development in Anna).
stories.impact: New luxury apartment development in Uptown Dallas signals sustained rental demand in the submarket and capital appetite for boutique, upscale products. The $100M price tag for 100 units (~$1M per unit) targets high rents ($2,500–3,500+) in a walkable, mixed-use corridor. Developers should monitor Uptown's supply pipeline and rent trends; strong demand metrics justify new luxury starts, but market saturation risk exists if too many 300+ unit projects deliver simultaneously. Cover2's multi-project portfolio (Wheelhouse, Greerhouse) validates young firm's credibility and access to capital; this is a proof-of-concept for emerging developer platforms in Texas.
